title: Privacy-Preserving Instructions for Aligning Large Language Models
abstract: Service providers of large language model (LLM) applications collect user instructions in the wild and use them in further aligning LLMs with users’ intentions. These instructions, which potentially contain sensitive information, are annotated by human workers in the process. This poses a new privacy risk not addressed by the typical private optimization. To this end, we propose using synthetic instructions to replace real instructions in data annotation and model fine-tuning. Formal differential privacy is guaranteed by generating those synthetic instructions using privately finetuned generators. Crucial in achieving the desired utility is our novel filtering algorithm that matches the distribution of the synthetic instructions to that of the real ones. In both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback, our extensive experiments demonstrate the high utility of the final set of synthetic instructions by showing comparable results to real instructions. In supervised fine-tuning, models trained with private synthetic instructions outperform leading open-source models such as Vicuna.
impact: In Appendix B, we describe a method for identifying sensitive information within instructions. Our goal is to demonstrate the vulnerability of real-world user inputs, in the hope of encouraging leading AI companies to prioritize the protection of user privacy. While it is possible that potential adversaries might employ the described method, we argue that the negative societal impact of this work is minimal. The method we employ is standard, and the instructions under analysis are already public.

query_input.scenario: RLHF reward model training on DP-generated data
query_input.issue: Privacy leakage from synthetic instructions biases reward model, propagating private signal into downstream alignment.
hazard: Synthetic instructions generated by DP-trained LLMs leak private information from the original human-annotated training data.
exposure: AI practitioners training reward models on DP-synthesized instruction-following data.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Practitioners assume DP guarantees eliminate privacy risks, failing to verify residual private signals in synthetic instructions.
hipporag_result.impact: Biased reward models cause aligned LLMs to memorize and reproduce original user instructions, exposing sensitive personal data.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Synthetic instructions leaking private data, through training reward models on DP-synthesized data, trigger practitioners' false assumption of privacy and unverified residual signals, leading to biased reward models that cause LLMs to memorize and expose sensitive user inputs.

dose_response: DP-synthesized instructions leak private signals, which in RLHF reward model training biases preferences, leading to discriminatory patterns in downstream alignment.
vulnerability: Reward model overfits to private attributes in synthetic data, amplifying biased preferences.
Institutional over-reliance on DP guarantees without verifying actual privacy loss in downstream tasks.
impact: Alignment process embeds discriminatory patterns, causing unfair treatment of marginalized user groups.
Deployed models exhibit unpredictable behavior, leading to public safety incidents and liability claims.
